text: <par>Mpi has two allofamic reflexes, one with palatal <latinform>ɲ-</latinform> and one with <latinform>h-</latinform>. The palatal (rather than velar) nasal is explicable in terms of the following high vowel, while the variation in initials reflects alternate simplifications of the complex initial <latinform>*ʔŋ-</latinform>.</par>
<par>RICE + HUNGRY. Or is it two versions of the same morph?</par>
<par>The final <latinform>-l</latinform> in this form is a tone mark.</par>
<par>final <latinform>-q</latinform> is a laryng. vowel, not a tone mark. This form marked “*3” in blue n.b.</par>
<par>2nd morpheme? PL: <latinform>sha˯ ji²</latinform> 1. meat, 2. game animal, 3. muscle; p.411.</par>
<par>The many binomes <xref ref="385">#385</xref> + <xref ref="1223">#1223</xref> demonstrate the distinctiveness of the two etyma.</par>
<par>the final -l is not a segmental phoneme, but a tonemark.,ie., nothing to do with the final in <reconstruction>*s/r-nil </reconstruction>'gums'.</par>

<par>In Paul Lewis' notation this would be <latinform>daw˯ dah˯</latinform>. We know that <reconstruction>*-aŋ</reconstruction> > Ak. <latinform>-ɔ</latinform> (PL -aw). But does <reconstruction>*-uŋ</reconstruction> > Ak. <latinform>-aŋ</latinform> (PL -ah) ? Compare the Sangkong form <latinform>toŋ³¹ taŋ⁵⁵</latinform> (150085) which reflects the same two roots in reverse order.</par>
<par>2d syll. was tagged with now-defunct #1106 <latinform>mak</latinform> BACK/LOINS/BODY/CORPSE.</par>
<par>M2 all this gp: 1384 or s? (1384 because of <latinform>maq</latinform>?)</par>
<par>BP pfx? or reduced from bø? M2: or 164?</par>
<par>M2: or 1390?= but prob. not: which makes me want to rethink ALL these 190s. (I did). Or should M2=M2 for all?</par>
<par>Hani final <latinform>-v</latinform> is a tonemark in this transcription.</par>
<par>Maran notes “from archaic sam-bang.”  Tones from Maran.</par>
<par>Judson p. 405 glosses as ‘hair of head’; Hanson cites (p.581) but gives no gloss.</par>
